module.title: Genre: Trap
module.description: Master the defining elements of trap music: 808 bass design, triplet hi-hat patterns, hard-hitting drums, and melodic elements. Build a complete trap beat from scratch using professional techniques.

lessons.title: The Trap Sound: 808s and Sub Bass
lessons.body:
The 808 bass is the defining element of trap music. Named after the Roland TR-808 drum machine's bass drum sound, the modern trap 808 is a long, pitch-sliding sub bass that doubles as both kick and bass — it carries the low end and the melody simultaneously. Getting it right is the difference between a trap beat that hits and one that feels weak.

## What the 808 Actually Is

The original TR-808 bass drum was a sine wave with a fast pitch drop. Trap producers took that concept and stretched it: long sustain, tuned pitch, and a slide from one note to the next. Modern 808s are typically made from:

- **Sampled 808s**: Pre-recorded 808 hits pitched in a sampler (FL Studio's Sampler channel, or DirectWave)
- **Synthesised 808s**: A sine wave oscillator with a pitch envelope, built in Sytrus or Harmor
- **Sample packs**: Thousands of 808 samples exist — select one that sits in the 40–80 Hz range for the root note

## Pitching and Tuning

The 808 must be **in key with your track**. An out-of-tune 808 is the most common beginner mistake — it sounds muddy and clashes with every other melodic element.

**Step 1 — Find the root note**: Play your 808 sample on a Sampler channel. Use a tuner plugin (or the built-in Pitch Correction in FL Studio) to identify what note the sample naturally plays at. Most commercial 808 samples are tuned to C.

**Step 2 — Pitch to your key**: In the Piano Roll, enter notes at the correct pitches for your chord progression. If your track is in F minor and the 808 root is C, you need to compensate: enter your F notes at the interval of a fifth above C (or adjust the sample's root note in the Sampler settings).

**Step 3 — Set the root in the Sampler**: In the Sampler channel properties, there is a **Middle C** (base note) setting. Set this to match the sample's actual tuning. Now every Piano Roll note will play the correct pitch.

## The 808 Slide

The signature trap 808 doesn't just play discrete notes — it *slides* from one note to the next, creating the melodic glide sound. In FL Studio:

1. Enter two adjacent 808 notes in the Piano Roll
2. Select the first note, right-click → **Properties**
3. Enable **Portamento** (or use the slide note function)
4. Alternatively, use FL Studio's **Slide** note type: hold Shift and click to draw slide notes

The portamento time controls how fast the pitch glides. Faster = snappier. Slower = more melodic legato feel. Typical trap 808 slides are 100–300 ms.

## Note Length and Decay

In trap, 808 notes are often long — they sustain through the beat and overlap into the next hit. The sample's **decay/release** in the Sampler controls how long it rings. For full sustain:

- Set the **Release** knob high in the Sampler
- Make the Piano Roll note longer than the grid step
- Use the volume envelope to shape the tail: a slight swell before a smooth fade avoids an unnatural chop

For a punchy, rhythmic 808 feel, shorten the notes and increase the attack on the volume envelope. The rhythm of the note lengths *is* the groove of the 808.

## 808 in the Mix

The 808 occupies the same frequency range as the kick drum. They will fight each other unless you manage the relationship:

- **Sidechain compression**: Duck the 808 volume slightly when the kick hits. Use Fruity Peak Controller or a dedicated sidechain compressor on the 808 channel. The kick punches through, then the 808 fills in behind it.
- **Layering**: Many producers layer a short punchy kick sample on top of the 808 to add transient click without losing the sub weight
- **HPF on the kick**: High-pass the kick sample slightly (80–100 Hz) so the sub frequencies are owned by the 808

## Common 808 Processing

| Plugin | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Parametric EQ 2 | Cut 200–400 Hz (mud), boost subtle 2–4 kHz for chest feel |
| Fruity Peak Controller | Sidechain ducking from kick |
| Fruity Waveshaper / Soft Clipper | Gentle saturation to add harmonics — makes 808 audible on small speakers |
| Fruity Blood Overdrive | More aggressive distortion for a gritty 808 character |

Saturation is particularly important: pure sine waves are nearly inaudible on laptop speakers. Adding subtle odd harmonics (via soft clipping) lifts the 808 into the midrange where small speakers can reproduce it, without losing the sub weight on systems with good low-end reproduction.

## Synthesising Your Own 808 in Sytrus

1. Load Sytrus → initialise to default
2. Set Operator 1 to a **sine wave**
3. On the OP1 envelope, set a very fast **Attack** (near 0) and a long **Release** (3–5 seconds)
4. Add a **Pitch envelope**: steep downward slope — fast drop from a high pitch to the note pitch. This is the 808 "thump" at the start
5. Adjust the pitch envelope depth to taste — more depth = more pronounced initial thump
6. Route to the main output and layer with a transient clap or click if needed

Building your own 808 gives you complete control over tuning and character, which is especially useful for making a signature sound.
keyTakeaways: The 808 must be tuned to match your track's key — set the correct base note in the Sampler properties and enter melodic pitches in the Piano Roll.
Use slide notes or portamento in the Piano Roll to create the characteristic pitch-glide between 808 notes.
Sidechain the 808 to the kick so both elements hit without fighting, and add subtle saturation so the sub is audible on small speakers.
lessons.tryThis: Load an 808 sample into a Sampler channel. Use a tuner to confirm its root note, then set that note as the Middle C in Sampler properties. Build a simple 4-bar progression in the Piano Roll — root, fifth, minor third, flat seventh. Enable slide notes between each pair. Add a Fruity Soft Clipper on the 808 mixer channel and gain-stage it until you can hear the 808 clearly through headphones at low volume. A/B with and without the soft clipper — notice how much the audibility improves without losing the sub weight.

lessons.title: Triplet Hi-Hat Programming
lessons.body:
The rolling triplet hi-hat is the most immediately recognizable element of trap production. The rapid, swinging 16th- and 32nd-note triplet runs give trap its nervous, hyperkinetic feel. Programming them convincingly requires understanding both the rhythmic grid and the performance variations that make them feel human.

## The Underlying Rhythm

Trap hi-hats operate in **triplet subdivisions** — groups of three evenly-spaced notes within a beat. The most common grid is **1/32 triplets** (also called 32T). In FL Studio's Step Sequencer and Piano Roll, you can switch the time signature or snap grid to triplet values:

- In the Piano Roll: press **Ctrl+G** to change the snap grid → select **1/32 (triplet)** or **1/16 (triplet)**
- In the Step Sequencer: click the **Step count** and choose 24 steps (which gives you triplet divisions within a 4/4 bar)

The simplest trap hi-hat pattern is a **straight roll**: 6 or 8 consecutive 1/32T notes run together. But the real craft is varying where the runs start, how long they are, and how the velocity changes within each run.

## Building in the Piano Roll (Recommended Method)

Working in the Piano Roll rather than the Step Sequencer gives you precise control over velocity, note length, and position.

**Step 1**: Draw a closed hi-hat note at bar 1, beat 1. Set length to 1/32.

**Step 2**: Copy it 5–7 times across a 1/32 triplet grid (three 1/32T notes = one quarter-note beat).

**Step 3**: Leave gaps. A trap hi-hat pattern has runs and silences, not constant straight 32nds. A typical 1-bar pattern might have: 2 notes, gap, 4-note run, gap, 6-note run, gap, 2 notes.

**Step 4**: Add velocity variation. Select all notes in a run, right-click → **Select all** → use the velocity lane at the bottom. Shape the run: softer at the start, louder toward the end (a swell), or louder on the first note then decaying. This is what makes the roll sound performed rather than quantized.

## Ghost Notes and Open Hi-Hats

Professional trap hi-hat patterns mix several types:

| Note Type | Velocity | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Closed hi-hat (main hits) | 80–110 | Rhythmic backbone |
| Ghost hi-hats | 20–50 | Fill space between main hits, add texture |
| Open hi-hat | 90–120 | Accents — usually on the offbeat or before a snare |
| Pedal hi-hat | 60–80 | Subtle close hits that add groove feel |

Open hi-hats ring longer. In a Sampler channel, use a longer note length or a separate open hi-hat sample. Many producers cut the open hi-hat with a closed hi-hat note immediately after — place a closed hi-hat note right after the open one to choke it.

## Velocity Automation: The Secret to Feel

The single biggest improvement you can make to a hi-hat pattern is velocity automation. Compare:

**Flat velocity (all at 100)**: Sounds mechanical and lifeless — the typical beginner pattern.

**Varied velocity**: Each run has a shape — perhaps building toward the end, or with an accent on beat 1 and 3 of the run, or with very low ghost notes between hits.

A useful approach is to imagine you are watching a drummer's hi-hat hand: the wrist movements create natural crescendos and decrescendos. The hand moves faster and harder as the roll intensifies, then relaxes. Replicate that arc in the velocity lane.

## Programming Multiple Hi-Hat Layers

Most trap producers use 2–3 hi-hat samples for a single pattern:

1. **Closed hi-hat sample** — tight, high-pitched, short
2. **Open hi-hat sample** — slightly longer ring, for accents
3. **Ride or shaker** — a different texture for contrast in fills

Program each on a separate channel in the Mixer. The closed and open should share the same Mixer track or be choked together so the open gets cut when a closed hits (simulating a real hi-hat mechanism).

## Common Trap Hi-Hat Patterns

**Basic trap roll** (1 bar, 1/16 grid with triplet feel):
```
Beat 1:  [X][X][X] . [X][X][X][X][X] . [X] .
Beat 2:  . [X][X][X][X] . [X][X][X] . . .
Beat 3:  [X][X][X][X][X][X] . . [X][X] . .
Beat 4:  . [X][X][X] . [X][X][X][X][X][X] .
```

**Fast 32-note trap fill**: A 6-note or 8-note run going straight into the snare on beat 3. The run builds velocity from 40 → 110, then cuts to silence on the snare hit.

## Swing and Humanization

Even with triplet quantization, fully quantized hi-hats can sound rigid. Add subtle humanization:

- **Select all hi-hat notes → right-click → Humanize**: FL Studio applies small random velocity and timing offsets
- Alternatively, draw in velocity variations manually — manual shaping always sounds better than algorithmic randomization
- **Timing**: Slightly pushing individual notes by 1–3 ticks forward gives a rushed, eager feel; pulling back by 1–3 ticks gives a laid-back pocket

## Processing Hi-Hats

- **High-pass filter**: 4–8 kHz HPF cuts mud, keeps hats crisp
- **Transient shaper**: Sharpen or soften the attack
- **Subtle reverb**: Very short room reverb (10–20 ms pre-delay, 0.3s decay) adds space without washing out the rhythm
- **Stereo width**: Spread hi-hats slightly wide in the stereo field — but keep the low end mono
keyTakeaways: Trap hi-hats use triplet subdivisions — switch your Piano Roll snap grid to 1/32 triplet and work in groups of three notes per beat.
Velocity variation is what makes hi-hat rolls feel performed: shape each run with a velocity arc rather than flat quantized values.
Layer closed, open, and ghost hi-hat notes for dimension — and use choke groups so open hi-hats are cut short when a closed hit follows.
lessons.tryThis: In the Piano Roll on a 1/32 triplet grid, program a single bar of hi-hats. Start with a 3-note run, leave a gap, then a 6-note run, another gap, and end with a 4-note run leading into bar 2. Now open the velocity lane and draw a velocity swell on the 6-note run: start at 30, rise to 120 by the last note. Listen back — notice how the run now feels like it's being performed rather than programmed. Repeat the bar 4 times and vary the run lengths each time.

lessons.title: Trap Drum Programming
lessons.body:
Trap drums are deceptively simple — sparse kicks, hard snares on beats 2 and 4, and a relentless hi-hat texture. But that simplicity is the point: every hit has space to breathe, which is what makes the 808 bass stand out. Getting the drum programming right means understanding where *not* to put notes as much as where to put them.

## The Trap Drum Foundation

A basic trap pattern in 4/4 at 130–145 BPM:

```
Kick:   [1] . . . . . . . [2.5] . . . [3.5] . . .
Snare:  . . . . [2] . . . . . . . [4] . . . .
Hi-hat: (triplet rolls throughout)
```

The kick and snare skeleton is minimal. The kick rarely lands on every beat — syncopation is critical. Landing on beat 1 plus an unexpected 16th-note early on beat 3 (the "and" of 2) is a classic trap placement.

## Kick Drum Programming

Trap kicks need weight and punch. Key techniques:

**Sample selection**: Use a kick with a hard transient click and a thump in the 60–80 Hz range. The click ensures it cuts through on small speakers; the low thump gives weight on systems with subwoofers.

**Layering**: Combine two kicks — a punchy "clicky" kick and a deeper "thuddy" kick. Layer them by placing identical notes on both Sampler channels simultaneously. Blend the mix so the click is louder than the thud (since the 808 covers the sub).

**Tuning the kick**: Tune the kick's pitch so it doesn't clash with the 808's root note. If the 808 is in F minor, the kick should either be close to F or harmonically neutral. Use the Sampler's pitch knob to adjust.

**Kick placement ideas**:
- Beat 1, syncopated hit before beat 3 (the "snare anticipation" feel)
- Double kick: two 16th notes in quick succession for aggression
- Ghost kick: soft kick on the "and" of beat 1 for groove

## Snare Drum Programming

The trap snare is hard, bright, and present — it cuts through the mix. Key choices:

**Main snare**: Loud, sharp crack. Lives at beats 2 and 4. Velocity should be high (110–127) for authority.

**Clap layering**: Layer a clap sample directly on top of the snare. The clap adds presence in the 2–6 kHz range. Slightly offset the clap by 3–5 milliseconds after the snare for a flam-like widening effect.

**Rimshots and ghost snares**: Program soft ghost snares (velocity 20–40) on off-beats for rhythmic texture without breaking the groove.

**Snare rolls**: A 16th-note run building into the main snare — typically 4 or 8 notes crescendoing from velocity 30 to 127. Place these before beat 3 or at the end of a phrase as a fill.

## Clap and Percussive Layering

Beyond the main kit, trap beats often include:

| Element | Position | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 808 clap | Beats 2 and 4 | Core backbeat alongside snare |
| Tambourine or shaker | Consistent 8th notes | Fills space between hi-hat rolls |
| Snap | Off-beats | Adds texture without competing with snare |
| Rim | Sparse placement | Contrast and ear candy |
| Tom hits | Phrase endings | Fills and transitions |

## Programming in the Step Sequencer vs Piano Roll

**Step Sequencer** (Beat + Bassline): Good for initial programming — lay down the kick, snare, and simple hi-hat foundation fast. Set to 16 or 32 steps for 16th-note precision.

**Piano Roll**: Essential for the hi-hat triplet rolls (as covered in Lesson 2) and for snare rolls and ghost notes. You get velocity editing, note length control, and position control.

Most producers start in the Step Sequencer, then move the pattern to the Playlist and switch to the Piano Roll for detailed hi-hat programming.

## Velocity and Human Feel

All drum hits should have velocity variation. Even the main snare on beat 2 doesn't have to be exactly 127 every time — a slight variation (105, 118, 127, 112) across 4 bars adds life. Use the Piano Roll's velocity lane:

- **Kick**: 100–127 for main hits, 60–80 for ghost kicks
- **Snare**: 110–127 for main hits, 20–50 for ghosts
- **Hi-hat**: Wide range, 15–120, shaped by run arcs

## Swing and Groove

Trap often uses subtle swing on the hi-hats and ghost notes, while keeping the kick and snare straight. In FL Studio:

- In the **Step Sequencer**, adjust the **Swing** knob (the circular control at the top right) — 0 = straight, 50 = maximum shuffle
- In the Piano Roll, manually push/pull specific notes by 1–3 ticks to create an organic groove
- Alternatively, use a **Groove template** (right-click the Step Sequencer → Groove → choose a template) to apply a rhythmic feel from a preset

## Processing the Kit

Each drum element should have its own Mixer track:

- **Kick**: High-pass at 40 Hz, slight low-mid boost at 200 Hz for body, transient shaper for punch
- **Snare/Clap**: High-pass at 150–200 Hz, presence boost at 3–5 kHz, reverb send for snap (short room reverb, 0.4s)
- **Hi-hats**: High-pass at 5–6 kHz, subtle compression to even out rolls
- **Drum bus**: Mild bus compression (2–3 dB GR) to glue the kit, subtle distortion/saturation for excitement
keyTakeaways: Trap kick patterns are sparse and syncopated — resist the urge to fill every beat and give the 808 and kick room to breathe.
Layer kick + clap on the snare hits and use ghost snares at low velocity to add groove depth without cluttering the main backbeat.
Process each drum element on its own Mixer track and bus the full kit through a single bus for glue compression.
lessons.tryThis: Build a 2-bar trap drum pattern using only kick, snare, and clap (no hi-hats yet). Program the kick at beats 1 and 2.5. Put the snare and clap on beats 2 and 4. Listen to how much space that creates. Now add 4 ghost kick notes at low velocity (around 50) in between the main kicks. Notice how the groove deepens without getting busy. Finally, offset the clap by 4 ms after the snare on every hit — compare with and without the offset.

lessons.title: Melodic Trap: Keys, Bells, and Pads
lessons.body:
Melodic trap elevated the genre from pure street rap to a mainstream phenomenon. The dark, emotional lead melodies — piano phrases, bell arpeggios, string pads — create the atmospheric backdrop that defines the contemporary trap sound. This lesson covers how to write and produce those melodic elements in FL Studio.

## The Emotional Vocabulary of Trap Melodies

Trap melodies tend toward a specific emotional quality: **dark, brooding, cinematic, with moments of melodic brightness**. The scales and chords that produce this feeling:

**Minor scales**: The natural minor scale is the foundation. For a trap beat in F minor, the notes F G Ab Bb C Db Eb create the characteristic dark-but-melodic feel.

**Minor pentatonic**: Simpler, more direct. F Ab Bb C Eb. Five notes that almost always work. The skip from the minor third to the fourth creates a soulful tension.

**Phrygian mode**: The flat 2nd degree (Db in F minor context) is especially dark. Melodies that land on the flat 2 have a cinematic, almost horror-film quality — used heavily in drill and darker trap.

**Chromatic passing notes**: Moving between scale degrees via half-steps creates a jazzy, unstable feel that resolves satisfyingly.

## Instrument Choices

The key instruments in melodic trap:

| Sound | Texture | FL Studio Source |
|---|---|---|
| Electric piano / Rhodes | Warm, slightly dirty, mid-forward | FLEX (Rhodes presets), Harmor |
| Plucked bells / Mallets | Bright, short attack, long ring | FLEX (Bell, Marimba presets) |
| Choir / Voice pad | Wide, atmospheric, legato | FLEX, Sytrus (pad presets) |
| Strings | Cinematic sweep | FLEX (String Ensemble), Harmor |
| Guitar (single notes) | Lo-fi, emotional | FLEX, sample packs |
| Flute | Bright, ethnic feel | FLEX (Flute presets) |

Many trap producers use **sampled loops** rather than programming from scratch — a vinyl soul record chop, a jazz piano sample, or a looped chord stab. But understanding how to program from scratch gives you the ability to create exactly what the track needs.

## Writing a Trap Melody

**Step 1 — Establish the tonal centre**: Start your first note on the root or the fifth of your key. The listener's ear anchors to this as home.

**Step 2 — Move in small intervals**: Trap melodies tend to step between adjacent scale degrees rather than large jumps. A melody that moves F → Eb → C → Db → Eb → F has more character than one that jumps around wide intervals.

**Step 3 — Leave space**: Trap melodies breathe. A note followed by a rest — especially before the 808 or snare hits — creates anticipation. Don't fill every 16th note.

**Step 4 — Repeat with variation**: Phrase a 2-bar melody, then repeat it with a small change on bar 2 (a different ending note, a chromatic approach, a rhythmic displacement). This creates memorability without monotony.

**Step 5 — Call and response**: Many trap melodies work in pairs — a high register phrase answered by a lower register response, or a bell melody answered by a pad sustain.

## Piano Roll Techniques for Trap Melody

**Velocity shaping for expression**: In a piano phrase, the first note of each group is accented (high velocity), the following notes softer. This gives a natural articulation. Shape velocity in the velocity lane — accent beat 1 of each bar at 110, melody notes at 70–90, grace notes at 40–60.

**Sustain and overlap**: For legato-style sounds (pads, strings), overlap consecutive notes in the Piano Roll — draw them so they start before the previous one ends. This creates smooth legato transitions.

**Staccato for bells**: For plucked bells, shorten note lengths to 1/16 or 1/32 even when the note is placed on a 1/4 boundary. The short note triggers the attack transient; the sample's natural decay handles the sustain.

**Pitch bend for expression**: Add a MIDI pitch bend curve in the Piano Roll (right-click → **Insert → Pitch curve**) to create subtle vibrato or a slide into a note.

## Chord Stabs and Pads

Beyond the lead melody, trap uses chords for harmonic colour:

**Minor 7 chords**: Stacked thirds — root, minor third, fifth, minor seventh. In F minor: F Ab C Eb. These have an R&B/soul warmth that complements the harsh drums.

**Diminished chords**: Create tension. Use sparingly on the IV or VII chord of a progression for momentary unease.

**Sustaining pads**: Held chords in the background fill out the low-mid frequency range that the 808 and kick leave vacant (roughly 200–800 Hz). Use a pad sound in FLEX with a slow attack so it swells in rather than attacking sharply.

## Layering for Width and Depth

A single piano note in mono sounds thin. Layering creates the wide, full sound of professional trap production:

1. **Lead + octave double**: Play the lead melody, then copy the Piano Roll channel and pitch it up an octave. Pan the original slightly left, the octave slightly right. Now the melody has width and body.
2. **Lead + bell layer**: Add a bell sound on a second channel playing the same notes. Bell + piano = a hybrid timbre that cuts through the mix with character.
3. **Reverb send for space**: Run the melody through a reverb return channel. Use a hall or plate reverb with 2–3s decay and 30 ms pre-delay. Keep the wet signal lower than the dry.
keyTakeaways: Trap melodies use minor and pentatonic scales for their dark emotional quality — move in small intervals, leave space, and build memorability through repetition with small variation.
Layer a bell or plucked sound on top of a piano lead to create the hybrid timbre characteristic of professional trap melodies.
Use legato overlap for pad sounds and short note lengths for bell/pluck sounds — note length in the Piano Roll controls the articulation of each instrument type.
lessons.tryThis: Load a Rhodes-style piano in FLEX and a bell or mallet sound on a second channel. In the Piano Roll (snap to 1/16), write a 2-bar F minor melody using only the pentatonic scale (F Ab Bb C Eb). Keep the rhythm simple — 6 to 8 notes across 2 bars. Now copy the Piano Roll to the bell channel, pitch it up an octave, and pan the piano slightly left and the bell slightly right. Add a reverb send at about -12 dB wet. Play your melody over a simple drum loop and 808 pattern — notice how the layering immediately sounds bigger and more professional.

lessons.title: Mixing the Trap Beat
lessons.body:
A well-written trap beat can still sound amateur if the mix is unbalanced. Mixing trap has specific challenges: the 808 and kick occupy the same frequency space, the hi-hats are dense and bright, and the melodic elements need to sit behind the drums without disappearing. This lesson covers the essential mixing moves that separate professional trap production from bedroom demos.

## Gain Staging First

Before adding any EQ or compression, set levels with faders only. The goal is a well-balanced mix that peaks around -6 dB on the master before any master bus processing.

**Priority order for level setting**:
1. Set the kick level — this is your loudness anchor
2. Balance the snare relative to the kick (snare is usually 2–4 dB louder in trap)
3. Sit the 808 so its sub frequencies support the kick rather than fighting it
4. Set hi-hats — usually 6–8 dB below the kick
5. Add melodic elements underneath the drums

A common trap mix mistake is making the melody too loud. In finished trap beats, the drums dominate and the melody sits back in the mix — the listener *feels* the melody rather than being consciously aware of it. Reference professional tracks at matched loudness to calibrate your level expectations.

## The 808-Kick Relationship

This is the central mixing challenge in trap. Both elements compete for the 50–100 Hz sub range. Three solutions:

**Sidechain compression** (most common): Route the kick to a sidechain input on a compressor placed on the 808 channel. When the kick hits, the 808 ducks momentarily, then swells back up. Use a fast attack (1–5 ms), medium release (80–150 ms), and a moderate ratio (3:1 to 6:1). The kick punches cleanly through and the 808 fills in behind it.

**Frequency separation**: High-pass the kick at 60–80 Hz so it doesn't extend into the deepest sub range. Let the 808 own below 60 Hz. The kick still has plenty of punch from its 80–200 Hz midrange body.

**Volume automation**: For a more manual approach, automate the 808's volume — draw a short dip on each kick hit, then a return. This gives you precise control but takes more time.

## EQ by Element

**808 Bass**:
- High-pass at 30–35 Hz (infrasonic rumble, inaudible and wastes headroom)
- Gentle cut at 250–350 Hz to reduce muddiness
- Subtle boost at 1–3 kHz with a narrow bell to add chest feel on smaller speakers
- The saturation from Lesson 1 already adds upper harmonics — don't over-boost the top end

**Kick**:
- High-pass at 40 Hz (sub handled by 808)
- Small boost at 80–100 Hz for weight
- Cut at 200–400 Hz to reduce cardboard muddiness
- Boost or leave the 3–6 kHz transient click intact for punch

**Snare/Clap**:
- High-pass at 150–200 Hz (the 808 and kick cover below this)
- Slight cut at 400–800 Hz if the snare sounds boxy
- Boost at 2–5 kHz for snap and presence
- Air boost at 10–14 kHz for brightness

**Hi-hats**:
- High-pass aggressively at 5–8 kHz — hats should live in the top end only
- Gentle shelf cut above 16 kHz if they're too harsh

**Melody (piano/bells)**:
- High-pass at 150–200 Hz to keep the low-mid clean for the drums
- Cut competing frequencies if the melody fights the snare in the 2–5 kHz range
- Presence cut at 800–1500 Hz if the melody sounds honky

## Reverb and Space

Trap reverb should be controlled — too much wash muddles the tight rhythmic feel. Guidelines:

| Element | Reverb Type | Decay | Pre-delay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Snare | Room / Small Hall | 0.5–0.8s | 15–25 ms |
| Kick | Very short room or none | 0.3s | 5 ms |
| Melody | Plate or Hall | 1.5–2.5s | 30–40 ms |
| Pads | Large Hall or convolution | 2–4s | 20–30 ms |

The pre-delay on the melody reverb is important — it keeps the dry signal upfront (the note attacks cleanly) while the reverb tail creates space behind it.

## Compression on the Mix

**Drums bus compression**: A bus compressor on a drum group (kick + snare + hat sent to a single Mixer bus) with a 2:1 ratio, medium attack (30–50 ms), and auto-release can glue the kit together. Aim for 1–3 dB of gain reduction.

**Melody compression**: A limiter-style compressor (high ratio, fast attack/release) on the melody keeps it from sticking out on louder notes. Alternatively, use a volume envelope in the Piano Roll to level out the melody before it hits any compressor.

**808 compression**: Avoid over-compressing the 808 — it already has a natural volume shape from the sample's envelope. A limiter to catch peaks is usually enough.

## Stereo Width

Trap mixes are wide in the mid and high range, tight in the low end:

- **808 and kick**: Keep fully mono (hard center, no stereo widening)
- **Snare/clap**: Can be slightly widened with a short stereo delay (L/R at 8–12 ms offset) for a wider crack
- **Hi-hats**: Pan different hi-hat elements slightly left and right (e.g., closed hat at -15, open hat at +20) for a realistic spread
- **Melody**: Wide stereo — use the pan-and-octave layering from Lesson 4, plus a subtle stereo widener on the pad layer
- **Overall**: Use a mid-side tool on the mix bus to check that below 150 Hz is fully mono

## Reference Tracking

The most reliable way to calibrate your trap mix is to reference against commercially released trap beats. In FL Studio, load a reference track into a Mixer channel and match its loudness (use a LUFS meter). Toggle your mix against the reference and note:

- Is your 808 as present and weighty?
- Are your hi-hats as bright and cutting?
- Does the melody sit back as far as in the reference?
- Is the overall stereo width comparable?

Make adjustments until your mix competes sonically.
keyTakeaways: Sidechain the 808 to the kick using a compressor — fast attack, medium release — so both hit cleanly in the same frequency range.
High-pass every element aggressively: melody at 150–200 Hz, hi-hats at 5–8 kHz, so each instrument lives only in its relevant frequency zone.
Keep the 808 and kick fully mono and widen only the mid-high elements — wide low end causes phase problems on mono systems and wastes headroom.
lessons.tryThis: Set up a sidechain on your 808: insert a Fruity Peak Controller or a compressor on the 808 mixer channel, routed from the kick. Set attack at 3 ms, release at 100 ms, ratio 4:1. Play your beat and listen as the kick punches through the mix. Now compare with the sidechain bypassed — notice how the kick and 808 fight without it. Adjust the release time: shorter release makes the 808 duck and swell dramatically (a pumping effect common in trap); longer release is more subtle. Find the setting where the kick hits clearly and the 808 feels continuous.

lessons.title: Complete Trap Beat Walkthrough
lessons.body:
This lesson brings everything from the module together into a single, complete trap beat — from the first sound to the finished, mixed result. Rather than isolated techniques, this walkthrough shows how the elements interact and how to make decisions in real time the way a working producer does.

## Starting Point: The Vibe

Before opening a plugin, define the emotional feel you're going for. Trap has many sub-moods:

- **Dark and aggressive**: Minor keys, heavy 808, hard drums, minimal melody
- **Emotional / melodic trap**: Minor melodies with more harmonic depth, longer 808 slides, softer drums
- **Hypnotic**: Repetitive looping phrases, gradual evolution, psychedelic feel
- **Cinematic**: Orchestra-style pads and strings, dramatic builds, big reverb

For this walkthrough, we'll build an **emotional melodic trap beat** at 140 BPM in F# minor.

## Step 1: The Sample or Sound Palette

Before drawing a single note, load the instruments that define the vibe:

1. **808 sample**: Load into a Sampler channel. Tune to F# (or set the base note in Sampler properties)
2. **Kick sample**: Punchy with click transient. Load second kick for body.
3. **Snare/clap**: Hard-hitting sample. Load a separate clap to layer.
4. **Closed hi-hat** and **open hi-hat**: Two separate channels
5. **Piano**: FLEX → browse to a warm electric piano or acoustic piano preset
6. **Bell layer**: FLEX → mallet or vibraphone preset
7. **Pad**: FLEX → warm pad preset in a minor tonality

Route everything to dedicated Mixer channels before starting. Naming the channels now saves time later: 808, Kick 1, Kick 2, Snare, Clap, Hi-hat C, Hi-hat O, Piano, Bell, Pad, FX Return 1 (reverb), FX Return 2 (reverb long).

## Step 2: Drum Foundation

Build in the Step Sequencer first:

**Kick pattern** (16-step, 1/16 resolution):
- Steps: 1, 5, 9, 13 (standard 4-on-the-floor) → delete 5 and 13, add step 7 (the "and" of beat 2 — syncopation)
- Final: Steps 1, 7, 9 with a ghost kick at step 3 (velocity 50)

**Snare/clap** (beats 2 and 4):
- Step 5 and step 13
- Add ghost snare at step 11 (just before beat 4), velocity 30

Copy the Beat+Bassline block to the Playlist and loop it for 8 bars.

## Step 3: Hi-Hat Layer

Switch to a dedicated Beat+Bassline (or Piano Roll) for the hi-hat. Set to 1/32 triplet resolution.

Program 2 bars of hi-hats using the techniques from Lesson 2:
- First bar: relaxed feel, shorter runs (3–4 notes)
- Second bar: builds with a 6-note roll going into bar 3

Shape velocities — no run should be flat. Every run has an arc.

## Step 4: The 808 Bassline

In the Piano Roll, programme a 4-bar 808 melody in F# minor:

```
Bar 1: F#2 (2 beats), slide to C#2 (2 beats)
Bar 2: B1 (1 beat), slide to D#2 (1 beat), E2 (2 beats)
Bar 3: Repeat bar 1
Bar 4: F#2 (1 beat), A1 (1 beat), B1 (2 beats) — different ending
```

Set note lengths to longer than the grid positions so they sustain and overlap. Enable slide notes between each pair. Sidechain to the kick (set up before listening).

## Step 5: The Melody

Programme the piano melody in the Piano Roll, 2 bars, F# minor pentatonic (F# A B C# E):

- Start on C#4 (the fifth above the root)
- Step down: C# → B → A → F# over the first bar
- Bar 2: ascending phrase F# → A → B, then a half-step neighbour to C → back to B
- Leave rests before beats 2 and 4 (where the snare hits) so the melody and snare don't collide

Copy to the bell channel, pitch up an octave, pan accordingly.

## Step 6: Pad Layer

Programme sustained chords under the melody:

- Bar 1–2: F# minor chord (F# A C#) held for 2 bars
- Bar 3–4: D major chord (D F# A) — the VI chord — creates emotional lift

Use a pad with a slow attack (attack = 400 ms) so the chord swells in rather than attacking. Volume at about -8 dB relative to the melody.

## Step 7: Arrangement (4-bar → 16-bar)

In the Playlist, lay out 4 pattern blocks:

| Bars | Content |
|---|---|
| 1–4 | Drums only (no melody — sparse, creates suspense) |
| 5–8 | Drums + 808 (the bass enters) |
| 9–12 | Full beat (drums + 808 + melody + bells) |
| 13–16 | Full beat + pad layer (maximum density) |

This 16-bar intro/build is a standard trap structure for the first section of a song.

## Step 8: Mixing the Beat

Apply the Lesson 5 mixing principles:

1. Gain stage — set all levels with faders, no EQ yet
2. Sidechain the 808 to the kick (3 ms attack, 100 ms release, 4:1 ratio)
3. High-pass everything above the 808: melody at 200 Hz, hi-hats at 6 kHz
4. Add reverb returns: short room on snare, medium plate on melody, large hall on pad (low level)
5. Apply the drum bus compressor (2:1, 30 ms attack, 2 dB gain reduction)
6. Set stereo widening — keep 808/kick mono, widen melody and bells slightly

## Step 9: Reference Check

Load a reference track into a Mixer channel. Match LUFS. A/B your beat against the reference:

- 808 comparison: Is it as weighty and present?
- Drum comparison: Is the snare crack as sharp and loud?
- Overall balance: Are the drums louder than the melody in the reference? Adjust yours to match.

## Step 10: Export

File → Export → WAV. Settings: 24-bit, 44.1 kHz. Name it with BPM and key for easy cataloguing: `Beat_140bpm_Fsharp_Minor_v1.wav`.

Congratulations — you've built a complete trap beat from scratch using professional techniques. The framework from this walkthrough applies to every trap beat you'll make: vibe → sounds → drums → 808 → melody → arrangement → mix → export.
keyTakeaways: Build trap beats in a deliberate order: drums → 808 → melody → pads → arrangement → mix. Each element informs the next rather than fighting for space.
Leave space early in the arrangement (drums-only bars) to build suspense — the full beat hits harder when the listener has been anticipating it.
The reference check at the end is not optional: your ears adapt to your own mix, and referencing resets your perspective to an objective standard.
lessons.tryThis: Follow the complete walkthrough above and build the full 16-bar beat. When you reach Step 9, load a professional trap track that you admire and A/B your beat against it at matched loudness. Write down three specific differences you notice (e.g., 'my 808 is muddier', 'my hi-hats are quieter', 'my reverb is washing out the snare'). Make those three adjustments and do one final A/B comparison. Keep this beat as your first reference mix for future trap production.
